title: Chapter 16: The Proposal
content: The champagne was Pol Roger, the vintage 2012, and it tasted like money — all mineral precision and toast-bubble effervescence that dissolved on the tongue before you could decide if you actually enjoyed it. Jane swirled the flute between her fingers and watched the strings of bubbles rise like tiny desperate things heading for air.

She'd been seated next to Edward for weeks now. At every dinner, every strategy session, every informal gathering in the west salon. What had once felt like strategic placement now felt like gravity — the simple, inevitable way her chair found his. That was the danger. Not him. The comfort.

The long table in the château's main dining hall glowed under iron chandeliers, their light softened by decades of beeswax. The walls wore their original fifteenth-century stone like a boast. Board members in dark suits occupied one end, senior staff the other, and the two visiting investors — a German venture capital director and her Swiss counterpart — held court in the middle, asking the kinds of questions that made Edward light up. He was in peak form tonight. The dark charcoal suit. The silver cufflinks that caught the candlelight. The voice that filled a room without rising.

"The IPO isn't an exit," Edward was saying, his hand moving in that practiced arc — generous, inclusive, drawing every eye. "It's an arrival. Thornfield has always been the most coveted name no one could own. Now they can. Paris will prove that."

He turned to Jane as he said it. He always turned to Jane. His eyes held something that looked terrifyingly like love — warm, certain, proprietary. The investors smiled. The board nodded. No one at the table questioned why the creative director was the axis around which Edward Rochester pivoted. They saw devotion. Jane saw choreography.
She lifted her champagne and drank. The bubbles caught in her throat, bright and barely painful, and she let them.

Dinner ended the way these evenings always did — with cognac and gradual migration toward the salon. But Edward didn't move. He remained standing at his place, and the room's current shifted, stilled, turned back toward him the way iron filings orient to a magnet.
He raised his glass.

"I want to thank everyone here tonight. The board, for your patience with my —" a self-deprecating pause, the kind that cost him nothing "— occasionally eccentric vision. Our investors, for seeing what we've always known: that Thornfield is the future of European luxury. And this extraordinary team, who make the impossible look routine every single day."
Murmurs of appreciation. Glasses lifted. Jane set hers down, a small stillness moving through her like a cold current.
Then Edward turned to her.
"There's one more thing."
His voice dropped a register — quieter, intimate, carrying the way only a voice trained for boardrooms and runways could carry. He reached into his jacket pocket.
The room went still.

Jane's heart didn't race. It calcified. It turned to something white and hard and ancient, a fossil of itself. Because she knew. She'd been reading his performance for weeks — the small increases in proximity, the way his hand lingered at the small of her back, the interviews where he described Thornfield's future and included her in every frame. She'd catalogued his tells the way he'd been cataloguing hers. She simply hadn't expected the move to come tonight, with witnesses, with investors, with no exit lane.
Edward dropped to one knee.

The sound of it — the soft impact of wool and bone against the stone floor — seemed impossibly loud. Every face in the room turned toward them. The German investor's lips parted. A senior staff member set down her cognac with a tiny clink.

"Jane Eyre." Edward's voice was raw in a way she'd never heard in a boardroom, and the performance of it was flawless. "You've changed everything. You've changed Thornfield. You've changed —" He paused. His throat moved. "— me. Will you marry me?"
He opened the box.

The ring was extraordinary. A vintage piece — 1920s, unmistakably — with a cushion-cut sapphire the color of a bruise, flanked by sharp Art Deco diamond shoulders. Platinum setting, milgrain edges, the whole thing speaking an aesthetic language so specific to Jane's private taste that it might have been pulled from a wish list she'd never written down.

Which meant he'd been studying her. Not watching — cataloguing. The art books she dog-eared in the library. The vintage jewelry sites in her browser history, which she'd been foolish enough to leave unguarded in a house where his security systems saw everything. He knew what she loved. He'd weaponized her own taste against her, wrapped it in platinum, and was offering it to her on his knee in front of nineteen witnesses.

The silence had weight. It pressed against Jane's chest, her throat, the backs of her eyes. Every face turned toward her. Every breath held. The investors watching with cautious interest. The board with varied expressions of surprise and corporate calculation. The staff with something closer to genuine wonder.

And Edward, on his knee, his face a portrait of vulnerable hope that she recognized, with the clinical precision of a woman who had been trained to see pattern in fabric and deceit in seam, as the most convincing performance of his career.

Jane's choice crystallized in a frozen second. A single fracturing moment behind her eyes where the math arranged itself with merciless clarity. Say no: she lost access to the atelier, to the financial records, to the basement. She lost her cover. She lost Bertha. Say yes: she stayed in the game. She gained trust, proximity, the keys to the architecture of Edward's empire. But she also bound herself to him publicly, legally, socially — tangled her life with the life of a man she was planning to dismantle. Marriage to a man she meant to destroy. The irony was so clean it nearly cut her.
"Yes."

The word came out steady. Almost soft. The room erupted — applause, exclamations, the bright pop of someone refilling a champagne flute in celebration. Edward's face broke into a smile that contained, Jane thought, genuine relief. He'd calculated her answer, but calculation wasn't certainty. He'd needed to be right.
He slid the ring onto her finger.
It fit perfectly.

The metal was cold at first — a shock against her warm skin — and then it began to warm, to become part of her hand's temperature, and that was worse. That was so much worse. Because it fit. Because it was made to fit. And as his hand closed over hers, the realization struck her like a needle through silk: he'd measured her. Some night while she slept in this château, in the room she'd begun to think of as safe, he'd come close enough to gauge the circumference of her ring finger. He'd pressed her hand or traced it or simply held it while she dreamed, and he'd done it with the same exacting precision he brought to a Thornfield collar measurement.
Her hand trembled once in his. He read it as emotion. He was always reading her, and he was always wrong in the exact way that served him.

Edward stood and pulled her into an embrace, and the room's applause swelled. Jane let herself be held. She felt the press of his chest, the warmth of him, the familiar scent of cedar and black pepper that she'd come to associate with danger dressed as desire. His lips brushed her ear — a touch so light it was almost not a touch at all, a ghost of contact that raised the fine hairs along her neck.
"I knew you'd say yes."
She turned her face into his shoulder. "Did you?"

She was smiling. She felt the smile on her face, the shape of it pressed against the wool of his suit, and the worst part — the part that calcified something else inside her, something beyond the heart — was that the smile was real. Not performed. Not calculated. Real warmth, real something, curving her lips against his collar. And Edward felt it. His arms tightened. He read it as joy.
It was something else entirely.

As he turned to accept handshakes and air-kisses, as the investors approached with champagne-ready congratulations, Jane's eyes moved across the room with the practiced scan of a woman who had learned to see while appearing to merely look.
She found Adèle.

The girl stood near the far wall, half-hidden behind a tall potted fern, and her face was a mask of horror. Not surprise — horror. The specific, knowing horror of someone who understood exactly what Edward Rochester was and what his proposal truly meant. Adèle's eyes were wide, her lips bloodless, her small hands gripping the fabric of her dress at the hip.
Jane held her gaze.
Slowly. Deliberately. Adèle gave the tiniest nod.
The game was on.

Edward's hand found the small of Jane's back, guiding her through the receiving line of well-wishers. She smiled. She accepted congratulations with grace that belonged to a woman she wasn't sure she still was. She let her hand be taken, the ring admired, the sapphire exclaimed over. She performed the role of the bride-to-be with the same meticulous care she brought to a Thornfield collection, and if her eyes kept drifting toward the fern by the far wall, no one noticed.
No one but Adèle, who had already disappeared.

The château slept. The last car had departed an hour ago, the investors driven back to their hotel in Tours, the board members to their respective flights. The staff had cleared the dining hall with quiet efficiency. The candles were out. The champagne flutes washed and stored.

Jane lay in her bed in the east wing, still dressed in the skirt and blouse she'd worn to dinner — she hadn't been able to remove them, hadn't been able to perform the ordinary rituals of ending a day. The ring sat on her finger, warm now, as if it had always been there. Its weight was wrong. Not heavy exactly, but present, in the way a handcuff is present even after you stop noticing the metal.
She reached for her laptop.

The maintenance log was an antiquated system — something left over from the château's pre-Thornfield days, a digital ledger for plumbing repairs and HVAC scheduling that no one had bothered to modernize. Jane and Bertha had discovered it months ago. It covered their tracks because no one in their right mind would search for clandestine communication in a record of boiler pressure readings and drainage snags.
She typed:
*East wing, Room 4B — persistent low pressure in radiator. Requires full system access for diagnostic. Clock starts now.*
Send.
She closed the laptop and lay back. Forty seconds. Then the screen lit with a new entry.
*Basement Level 2 — valve replacement scheduled. Will coordinate with wedding planning.*
A pause. A second message appeared.
*Then let's make it a wedding he'll never forget.*
Jane stared at the words glowing in the dark room. Then she closed the laptop and set it aside.

The ring was still there. Still warm. Still fitting perfectly. She held her hand up against the darkness and turned it, watching the sapphire catch the faint moonlight through the curtains. A bruise-colored stone. A wound in platinum.
I'm engaged to the man I'm going to destroy. In a château with a woman he caged. For a brand built on lies.
The ring fit perfectly.
That was the most terrifying part.
She was in. All the way in. And now there was no turning back.
